items.label: Scottie Scheffler (+270):
items.body: World No. 1 and clear betting favorite returning to his home state, with three prior top‑5s at Memorial Park and elite tee‑to‑green form that has translated into multiple recent wins on the PGA Tour.

items.label: Min Woo Lee (+2250):
items.body: Defending Texas Children’s Houston Open champion whose aggressive driving and creative shot‑making fit Memorial Park perfectly, coming in looking to repeat after last year’s narrow victory over a stacked leaderboard.

items.label: Sam Burns (+2450):
items.body: Proven winner on demanding par‑70/71 setups with a hot putter on firm, fast greens, giving him high-end win equity any time scoring hovers around single digits under par.

items.label: Chris Gotterup (+2450):
items.body: Big‑hitting rising star with a recent run of top‑20 finishes and one of the better strokes‑gained profiles in this field, especially off the tee and on approach at longer par‑4s.

items.label: Jake Knapp (+2600):
items.body: Breakthrough winner earlier this season whose combination of distance and fearless iron play has quickly established him as one of the Tour’s more volatile but dangerous outright targets.

items.label: Brooks Koepka (+2600):
items.body: Multiple‑time major champion making a stateside start on a course that rewards his preferred profile of high ball flight, strong mid‑iron play, and a proven ability to close in big‑time fields.

items.label: Rickie Fowler (+2900):
items.body: Rejuvenated veteran with a solid history on classical, tree‑lined layouts and an improving approach game that can put him firmly in the mix if the putter cooperates for four days.

items.label: Nicolai Hojgaard (+3500):
items.body: Explosive young talent with elite distance and birdie‑making ability, well‑suited to attacking Memorial Park’s par‑5s and long par‑4s if he keeps the driver in play.

items.label: Harry Hall (+3500):
items.body: One of the better putters in the field and a sneaky course‑fit whose steady ball striking and comfort on firm greens have already produced a top‑15 finish at Memorial Park.

items.label: Masters Lead‑In:
items.body: The Texas Children’s Houston Open serves as one of the final tune‑ups before the Masters, drawing a stronger‑than‑usual field as top players look to sharpen their games on a demanding championship layout.

items.label: Home‑State Favorite:
items.body: Scottie Scheffler returns to his home state of Texas as the world’s top‑ranked player and heavy betting favorite, still chasing a first Houston Open title despite multiple prior close calls at Memorial Park.

items.label: Defending Champion Pressure:
items.body: Min Woo Lee arrives as the defending champion after edging out Scottie Scheffler and Gary Woodland last year, attempting to go back‑to‑back at a venue that clearly fits his aggressive, creative style.

items.label: Depth of Contenders:
items.body: Behind Scheffler the outright market is tightly bunched, with a deep group of proven winners and recent breakout performers in the 20‑1 to 40‑1 range creating abundant mid‑tier betting value.

items.label: Recent Champions:
items.body: Memorial Park has produced a mix of winners, including Stephan Jaeger in 2024 and Min Woo Lee in 2025, with Scottie Scheffler posting multiple runner‑up finishes without yet lifting the trophy.

items.label: Course Tendencies:
items.body: Since relocating to Memorial Park, the Houston Open has generally rewarded all‑around tee‑to‑green excellence rather than pure bomb‑and‑gouge, with winners typically ranking highly in approach play and around‑the‑green performance for the week.

items.label: Scoring Profile:
items.body: Winning scores at Memorial Park have usually landed in the mid‑teens under par depending on wind and firmness, with difficult par‑4s and stout par‑3s keeping the event from becoming a pure birdie‑fest.

items.label: Venue:
items.body: Memorial Park Golf Course, Houston, Texas; a municipal layout redesigned for PGA Tour play that stretches over 7,400 yards and emphasizes demanding approach shots, challenging green surrounds, and strategic angles off the tee.

items.label: Course Setup:
items.body: Par‑70 championship test with only two par‑5s, several long par‑4s, and a set of tough par‑3s that collectively place a premium on mid‑to‑long‑iron precision and scrambling on tightly mown runoffs.

items.label: Purse:
items.body: Standard full‑field PGA Tour purse in the mid‑$9 million range, with the winner earning a seven‑figure payday along with valuable FedEx Cup points ahead of the spring major championship push.

items.label: Format and Dates:
items.body: 72‑hole stroke play with a 36‑hole cut to the low 65 and ties after Friday, contested this week from Thursday through Sunday in the heart of the 2026 PGA Tour regular season.

items.label: TV:
items.body: Golf Channel and NBC are expected to share U.S. television coverage, with early‑round and featured‑group streaming available via PGA Tour Live on ESPN+ for subscribers.

items.label: Thursday (Round 1):
items.body: Morning featured‑group coverage on PGA Tour Live followed by an afternoon TV window on Golf Channel showcasing early marquee groups at Memorial Park.

items.label: Friday (Round 2):
items.body: Streaming coverage continues from early tee times, with Golf Channel again carrying the afternoon television window as players battle to make the 36‑hole cut.

items.label: Saturday (Round 3):
items.body: Moving‑day coverage shifts more heavily to NBC in the afternoon while ESPN+ provides wall‑to‑wall streaming of leaders and key chasers jockeying for position.

items.label: Sunday (Final Round):
items.body: Final‑round broadcasts focus on the lead groups coming home on Memorial Park’s demanding closing stretch, with NBC handling the main TV window and PGA Tour Live offering full‑round streaming.
